title: The Top 10 Junk Sci-Fi Movies, ‘Moonfall,’ and Michael Mann’s ‘Ferrari’
format: A sequel to their 2020 garbage crime episode: Sean and Chris Ryan argue out the rules of junk sci-fi, then each induct five movies into a ten-film hall of fame, with quick stops for the Oscar nominations, Michael Mann's long-gestating 'Ferrari,' and capsule reviews of 'Moonfall' and 'Kimi.'

segments.heading: The Oscar Nominations
segments.note: on the eve of the 94th Academy Awards

films.title: Don't Look Up
films.note: the Kimmel comparison point — overwrought satire, not without laughs

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: left off the Best Picture list; the hypothetical snub they keep circling

films.title: The Power of the Dog
films.note: the grim, serious front-runner

films.title: Drive My Car
films.note: a Best Picture nominee neither host predicted

films.title: The Worst Person in the World
films.note: hoping the nomination sends people to see it

films.title: CODA
films.note: Sean hasn't seen it yet, but the nominations made him want to

films.title: Belfast
films.note: Branagh's nomination felt inevitable from day one

segments.heading: Michael Mann's 'Ferrari'
segments.note: Adam Driver, Penélope Cruz, and Shailene Woodley join a 20-years-gestating passion project

films.title: Ferrari
films.note: Driver plays Enzo Ferrari; Mann's long-delayed dream project finally has a cast

films.title: House of Gucci
films.note: Driver stayed in Italy — and in the accent — straight off this one

films.title: Ford v Ferrari
films.note: once a Michael Mann project before it went to James Mangold

films.title: Heat
films.note: the movie they say their whole garbage-crime bit flows from

segments.heading: 'Moonfall' and 'Kimi'
segments.note: Emmerich's disaster epic vs. Soderbergh's techno-anxiety thriller

films.title: Moonfall
films.note: bad, per Sean — but a great VOD rental

films.title: Kimi
films.note: minor Soderbergh, but a fun genre dip; a strong Zoë Kravitz performance

segments.heading: What Counts as Junk Sci-Fi
segments.note: the ground rules, argued out before the list starts

films.title: Gattaca
films.note: Andrew Niccol's debut — too highfalutin to be garbage

films.title: The Truman Show
films.note: Niccol wrote it

films.title: S1m0ne
films.note: Niccol's Al Pacino robot movie — called "Simone" on air

films.title: Lord of War
films.note: Niccol again, not sci-fi

films.title: In Time
films.note: Niccol's time-as-currency movie — right on the edge of garbage

films.title: Looper
films.note: too much of an indie-drama sensibility to be garbage

films.title: Chronicle
films.note: same verdict, different director — Josh Trank

films.title: Midnight Special
films.note: Jeff Nichols — too philosophically driven

films.title: The Guest
films.note: John Carpenter homage, but too A24-cool to be garbage

films.title: Network
films.note: raised as a satire test case — ruled a masterpiece, not garbage

films.title: Dr. Strangelove
films.note: same conversation, same verdict

films.title: Alien³
films.note: "industrial goth metal sci-fi," not garbage

films.title: Alien Resurrection
films.note: gloopy, junky, neon-green production design — this one's garbage

films.title: Alien: Covenant
films.note: has garbage elements but gets pulled into the bigger mythology

films.title: Sunshine
films.note: beautiful sci-fi for the first half, garbage once the demon shows up

films.title: Mission to Mars
films.note: junky, but sincere about the search for life — not garbage

films.title: Red Planet
films.note: junk, unlike its 2000 Mars-movie twin

films.title: The Matrix
films.note: the test case for how much "terrestrial" sci-fi even counts

films.title: Blade Runner
films.note: cited as future film noir, not garbage

films.title: Strange Days
films.note: same lineage as Blade Runner

films.title: I, Robot
films.note: an Alex Proyas credit up for debate

films.title: The Crow
films.note: same director, same question mark

films.title: Dark City
films.note: probably qualifies, despite the Ebert rave

films.title: Knowing
films.note: same Proyas question mark

films.title: Gods of Egypt
films.note: same

films.title: Starship Troopers
films.note: "the most important film in this conversation" — ruled not garbage because the fascism satire was the intent, not a later reading

films.title: Total Recall
films.note: Verhoeven — an out-and-out classic, not garbage

films.title: RoboCop
films.note: same Verhoeven defense

films.title: The Running Man
films.note: allowed in — future dystopia is enough, even light on tech

films.title: Aliens
films.note: the source of the Paul Reiser corporate-suit archetype they keep referencing

films.title: Predator
films.note: cited while laying out the genre's beats

films.title: Predator 2
films.note: preferred over the original as junk sci-fi

films.title: The Core
films.note: the platonic ideal of the "the" + one noun title

films.title: The Cell
films.note: wanted to be the new Alien, wasn't

films.title: The Island
films.note: too good for garbage sci-fi, per both hosts

films.title: The Arrival
films.note: David Twohy, Charlie Sheen — the garbage one, not to be confused with Villeneuve's film

films.title: Arrival
films.note: explicitly not garbage — the film they're distinguishing The Arrival from

films.title: The Fifth Element
films.note: not garbage, but the root of a whole vaudevillian strand of '90s sci-fi costume design

films.title: Battlefield Earth
films.note: widely derided source material, cited as a genre hallmark

films.title: The Butterfly Effect
films.note: the titled-after-its-own-phenomenon example

segments.heading: The Junk Sci-Fi Hall of Fame
segments.note: five picks each — Sean and Chris overlapped on only one

picks.title: Event Horizon
picks.note: the shared No. 1, "the alpha and the omega" — Sam Neill and Laurence Fishburne anchor a movie that turns fully feral two-thirds through

picks.title: Stargate
picks.note: Kurt Russell and James Spader crack open a portal to another dimension; a $200M hit that's since been half-forgotten

picks.title: Ghosts of Mars
picks.note: Rio Bravo in space — Natasha Henstridge, Ice Cube, and Jason Statham fighting possessed Mars colonists

picks.title: Cube
picks.note: six strangers wake up trapped in a deadly cube on a $350,000 budget

picks.title: Freejack
picks.note: Emilio Estevez's soul gets evicted by Anthony Hopkins in a dystopian near-future L.A.

picks.title: Species
picks.note: Natasha Henstridge's alien-human hybrid, designed by H.R. Giger, hunting for a mate

picks.title: Underwater
picks.note: Kristen Stewart does Sigourney Weaver eight miles under the ocean

picks.title: Congo
picks.note: Michael Crichton's talking gorillas and a laser-beam shootout in the finale — Sean's favorite on the whole list

picks.title: Pandorum
picks.note: Dennis Quaid and Ben Foster wake from deep sleep with amnesia, then the aliens show up

films.title: Sphere
films.note: another Crichton adaptation — Dustin Hoffman investigating a spacecraft at the bottom of the ocean

films.title: Predators
films.note: Adrien Brody, Topher Grace, Walton Goggins, and Mahershala Ali dropped on a planet to fight Predators

films.title: Johnny Mnemonic
films.note: a precursor to The Matrix, adapted from beloved source material into a mediocre movie

films.title: Judge Dredd
films.note: the Stallone version — could've been better

films.title: Screamers
films.note: Peter Weller vs. underground burrowing robots on a distant mining planet

films.title: Timecop
films.note: Van Damme's significant entry in the genre

films.title: Planet of the Apes
films.note: the Tim Burton version — "more trash than sci-fi," and the one that counts here, unlike the original '60s/'70s films or the Matt Reeves trilogy

films.title: Leviathan
films.note: Peter Weller again — a monster at the bottom of the ocean; Chris's runner-up underwater pick

films.title: Supernova
films.note: Walter Hill's movie, recut by Francis Ford Coppola after a disastrous test screening

referenced.group: Roger Donaldson filmography, run down live
Michael Crichton's other adaptations, mentioned in passing
John Carpenter films ruled not garbage
Paul W.S. Anderson's other genre films
